title: Scaling and network evolution of technology transfer in US cities
core_conclusion: Across 166 years (1858-2023) of US patent records, technology transfers scale superlinearly with city population, with exponents ranked intracity transfer > intercity transfer-in > intercity transfer-out. The transfer network's evolution combines nationwide space-filling, an emerging core-periphery hierarchy among cities, and rising functional polycentricity within cities; these dynamics grow in tandem with the scaling exponents, linking urban scaling laws to network evolution and to local vs. nonlocal knowledge interactions.
why_it_matters: First systematic scaling law for technology transfer (the bridge from knowledge to economic value); unifies static urban scaling with dynamic network evolution; empirically distinguishes local (intracity) from nonlocal (intercity) knowledge recombination; offers a rare 166-year deep-time view of an urban system filling, hierarchizing, and polycentrifying.
why_nature: Universal/system-level law over single case; cross-disciplinary bridge between urban scaling physics and complex-network evolution; distinctive 166-year large-scale empirics; advances evolutionary theory of complex urban systems with innovation- and regional-development policy resonance.
added_value_to_my_research: Inequality decomposition of a scaling exponent -> spatial-entropy quantification of hub-driven advantage
Density-driven space-filling generator -> null model for agent-based service/job-housing network growth
In/out/intra transfer signatures -> differentiated city-role taxonomy for intercity healthcare and telemedicine
Temporal GNN surrogate with scaling constraint -> explainable physics-informed forecasting of intercity service networks
Transfer-network features as predictors -> explainable-ML attribution of knowledge structure to economic/health outcomes
